text: I usually have toast with avocado and scrambled eggs, maybe some fresh fruit on the side. If I'm feeling fancy, I might whip up some scones with jam and cream while my tea is brewing. Simple, comforting food really helps me start the day off right. Nothing too fussy, mind – just something easy to digest so I can be ready to tackle whatever comes up!

I usually have a simple lunch at my kitchen table, really. My daughter comes round occasionally and we might share some sandwiches, soup, or leftovers from last night's dinner. If I'm feeling peckish before meeting someone, I'll grab something quick like a sandwich from the deli down the road – a bit pricey, but convenient. On Fridays, I sometimes meet friends at our local café for a chat over a cuppa and cake; those always bring me out of my comfort zone a bit! Usually, though, Monday to Thursday is just a bowl of cereal or leftover pasta from Sunday.

I tend to be in bed by 10 pm these days. Getting older does seem to catch up with me a bit more at night! It's hard to explain why, but once my son has gone out for his evening engagement, and we're all settled in, I find myself winding down rather quietly before drifting off. Still, I'm getting there – been told I should try an earlier bedtime anyway...

I try to start my day with a gentle morning routine – a cuppa tea, maybe some reading before getting out into the garden to tend to my vegetables or flowers. It helps me feel more centered and prepared for the day ahead. As I'm a bit of an early riser, I like to have some quiet time to myself before anyone else wakes up. After breakfast, I might take another walk round the block if the weather permits. A bit of fresh air never hurts! Of course, there are days when energy levels flag, so I make sure to listen to my body and rest when needed. Overall, taking care of oneself is crucial to enjoying life, isn't it? And I believe having simple pleasures like nature and good company really contribute to overall well-being.

Well, I think networking is a bit different now compared to my younger days, isn't it? In our trusty old GP surgery where I used to work, we didn't really have all these digital platforms and whatnot. We relied more on face-to-face interactions and word-of-mouth recommendations. You'd catch up with colleagues during breaks, share tips and advice, attend training sessions together...that sort of thing. Nowadays, I suppose everyone relies heavily on emails and LinkedIn connections. My son helps me set up meetings and introduces me to people he knows who might be interested in meeting someone like me – an older woman with some experience in admin and medical backgrounds. It's nice to connect with others in similar fields, even if we're not exactly in the same line of work anymore. Occasionally, I'll pop into local events related to healthcare or volunteer groups, which allows me to meet fellow retirees who share similar passions. It feels lovely to chat with them about shared interests rather than just exchanging CVs! But overall, I'm not one for grand schemes or forcing myself out there; I prefer quieter, genuine relationships built on mutual respect and interest.

Well, I'm a morning person, so I find it easier to focus during my most productive hours between 8 am and 12 pm. To be honest, I sometimes struggle with distractions later in the day, especially around 2-3 pm after lunch. So, what works best for me is having a dedicated workspace set up at home, free from interruptions, where I can settle into work mode. A cuppa, some fresh air outside, and maybe a nice bit of music to listen to all help too! It really does make a big difference. And I must admit, I still try to stick to an actual daily schedule rather than letting the day drift – even though I don't have anyone above me to report to now that I'm fully retired, getting into a rhythm helps me feel more organised and focused. Plus, being able to take breaks whenever needed allows me to recharge and come back refreshed. Simple things like taking a walk round the block mid-day, chatting with neighbours, or catching up on some paperwork before settling in again seem to suit me fine.

I'm still getting used to them, to be honest. My son helped me set one up a couple of years ago so my grandkids could call me more easily. It's been quite convenient really. But sometimes I find myself fumbling with the equipment and wishing someone was there to show me how again! Still, it's lovely catching up with the lads and girls like that way. And the kids love being able to chat to Nana in real-time. Would I prefer an old-fashioned phone call? Yes, perhaps...but we're all adapting, aren't we?

Staying motivated without an office structure is definitely a challenge! For me, having a bit of a daily routine helps. I like starting my morning quite early, usually around 7 o'clock, and getting some exercise – maybe a walk round the block or some stretching exercises. It gets the blood flowing and sets a sense of purpose for the rest of the day. After that, I try to tackle any pressing tasks first thing, so I'm feeling productive before the afternoon slump hits. And speaking of which, I find that taking breaks is essential too. A nice cuppa and a biscuit always seem to perk me up! But what really motivates me is knowing what I need to achieve each day. Writing down my priorities and checking them off one by one gives me a great sense of satisfaction. Plus, I love spending time with my grandkids, whether we're baking together or playing games. They bring such joy into my life, and being involved in their lives makes every day feel worthwhile. It might sound simple, but it works for me! Of course, there will be days when motivation falters, but overall, I think it's all about finding your rhythm and making the most of the freedom that retirement brings. As I say, "patience and

I find that my experience has taught me to be quite flexible when it comes to prioritising tasks. In my previous roles, I had a lot of responsibilities, so I'd have to juggle multiple projects simultaneously. You know, managing a busy office like an education setting requires being able to adapt quickly. When something urgent pops up, whether it's an emergency meeting or an unexpected query from a parent, you need to adjust your focus accordingly. Now that I'm fully retired, I still try to bring some discipline into my daily routine – waking up at 6:30 am helps set the tone! But since retirement, I actually appreciate having more flexibility to shift priorities based on how I feel each morning. If I'm feeling energised, I'll tackle those big tasks first; otherwise, I might start with something smaller. It really depends on what works best for me on any given day. It's funny, sometimes I think people assume because I'm retired, I must be bored out of my mind. But honestly, there's plenty to keep me occupied, even now. Whether it's tending to my little vegetable patch, catching up with grandkids, or getting involved in local charity initiatives...life is never dull! So while adapting to changing circumstances isn't always

I think being an older person in a relatively senior position has helped me develop my confidence in speaking up for myself. When there's something that concerns me or affects how I do my job, I'll usually speak directly with whoever is relevant – whether it's a colleague or our manager. If needed, I might escalate it to someone higher up. I find that having a gentle yet assertive tone helps me convey my thoughts effectively. It's essential to be clear about what I'm saying while still showing respect for others' opinions and authority. My priority is always finding solutions rather than causing unnecessary conflict.

I think when I feel like I'm being misunderstood, my first instinct is to try and clarify what was said or meant, rather than jumping straight into a defensive response. You know, take a deep breath, put on my reading glasses, and have another go at understanding where there might be a misstep. If that still doesn't work, I'll usually ask someone to explain themselves again, maybe rephrase or elaborate so we're all on the same page. Sometimes, I'll even admit when I haven't understood something myself – it shows vulnerability, but helps us move forward. And remember, I believe patience and curiosity always got me further in life!

I usually try to be a bit of an anchor, keeping everyone grounded while we're all catching up. You know, sharing some stories, asking how everyone's been getting along. My grandkids love having me make them my famous scones or cookies; they always look forward to those little treats. And I have to say, our conversations can get quite lively – my daughter likes to tease me about still being set in my ways, while my son rolls his eyes good-naturedly... It's lovely to spend time together like that, even if we might not always agree!

To be honest, I'm not too keen on these modern platforms like LinkedIn. As a retiree, my focus is more on staying connected with family and keeping up-to-date with local news rather than networking professionally. My son helps me set up and manage our family profiles occasionally, so he handles most of that side of things. I'd much prefer a simple, traditional way of communicating, like an email or letter – there's something comforting about putting pen to paper! If someone wants to connect with me directly, we usually exchange emails or meet for a cuppa instead of going digital.

I think I'd always try to be honest and straightforward, while still being considerate of other people's feelings. If someone asks me a personal question or shares something private with me, I'll listen attentively and respond thoughtfully. I believe it's essential to show empathy and validate their emotions, even if we might not agree on everything. However, I'm not one for beating around the bush – if there's an issue needing addressing, I'll usually speak my mind clearly and respectfully, making sure my tone is gentle yet firm.

I'd say I'm quite straight-forward and honest, so I tend to avoid unnecessary gossip or drama whenever possible. If someone tries to persuade me into doing something I'm not comfortable with, I'll usually politely decline and explain my reasons why. I value being true to myself and others, rather than conforming solely for the sake of fitting in. Of course, there have been instances where I may bend a little to accommodate close friendships or familial relationships, but ultimately, I try to prioritize what feels authentic to me. It's easier said than done sometimes, though!

I'm quite wary of those targeted adverts, to be honest. It feels like they know so much about me already! Sometimes I'll block them altogether, other times I might unblock certain websites I find interesting. To be fair, my grandsons always tease me about being a "technophobe" because I struggle with updating my devices or keeping up with all these new-fangled gadgets, but I suppose that's just part of getting older! What really gets my goat is when companies try to sell something I never asked for – usually some sort of subscription service. A simple ad should say what it wants before asking permission, rather than popping up suddenly and expecting an automatic yes. Still, I suppose it's a necessary evil; better that way than having annoying pop-ups everywhere.

Well, I must admit I'm still getting used to all these new-fangled devices! To be honest, my grandsons helped me set everything up, so he does most of the tech stuff now. But usually, I stick to what feels like a simplified interface – mainly using a mix of Google Chrome and Safari. If there's too many open at once, I tend to close them one by one until I find what I need. And sometimes I'll ask him to show me how to tidy them up properly. It gets overwhelming with so much clutter everywhere! A clean workspace is essential for an organised mind, don't you think?

I try to ignore them most of the time, really. It's easy to let someone else's negativity bring you down, isn't it? But I find that focusing on my own work and staying busy helps me block out all the noise. If I'm being completely honest, though, there have been a few instances where a comment has gotten under my skin – perhaps because it was unfair or unjustified. In those cases, I'll usually respond politely but firmly, explaining why what they said wasn't acceptable. I believe it's essential to stand up for oneself, even if it feels uncomfortable sometimes. After all, silence is rarely an option, and I'd rather set the record straight than let hurtful words go unchallenged. My husband always used to say that I should rise above such petty nonsense; he had a way of putting things into perspective. Now that he's gone, I think his advice still holds true.

When we have issues with our video call setup, my grandchild usually comes to sort it out for me. If he's busy, I'll ask one of them to come round instead. Alternatively, I might wait until someone else is available before calling again. It gets a bit frustrating, but I'm grateful to have such helpful kids who know how to troubleshoot these sorts of things!

I find it quite simple, really. As a saver and someone who likes to stick to routines, I always make sure to set reminders on my calendar two weeks before each bill is due. Then, I take some time during the morning – usually while having my tea – to go through all my papers and sort out which ones need payment, how much I owe, and what date it falls due. Once I have everything sorted, I pay them directly into our bank account using my debit card or standing order, whichever suits best. It gives me peace of mind knowing I'm staying on top of things. And, of course, keeping track of any changes in payments, like council tax or utility rates, helps prevent any mistakes. I suppose one could say I rely more on instinct now since I haven't needed to be too fussy once retirement came along!

I always try to think whether there's any real value in keeping a particular subscription before deciding what to cancel. It might be something like an expensive streaming service we never end up using much, or perhaps a magazine that's been sitting unread on my shelf for months. If I ask myself "would I miss it if it was gone?" and the answer is yes, I'll consider retaining it; otherwise, I'm happy to let it lapse. My grandkids find it amusing how tidy I am with our subscriptions – probably due to having grown up during austerity!

I like to take my time when considering a major purchase. It's always been taught me that "a penny saved is a penny earned" growing up, so I try to make sure we really need something before spending money. My husband used to say that too; he was quite the thrifty one himself. Now, since his passing, I tend to be even more mindful of our finances. We have a rough idea of what we're looking for, but I still think it's wise to weigh the pros and cons, read reviews, compare prices... all that sort of thing. If it'll bring us joy or improve our lives, great! But only if we're being sensible about it. And of course, I'd discuss it with my grandkids – they love involving themselves in decision-making processes, and it helps them develop some valuable lessons along the way.

I think I'd have to say that I'm a bit of an opportunistic buyer, really. If something is available at a reasonable price and seems like a good deal, I'll consider purchasing second-hand. But if there's any doubt or uncertainty, I tend to lean towards buying brand-new whenever possible. For me, reliability and durability are key – I hate the thought of having to repair or replace something frequently because it was bought cheaply initially. Plus, some products seem to hold their value better when purchased new, so I figure it might be worth paying a little extra upfront rather than taking risks with pre-owned items. Of course, my husband always said I was more frugal than necessary, but he was usually joking! Anyway, as long as it makes sense financially, I try to trust my instincts and make decisions based on what feels right for me.

I think my upbringing had a lot to do with teaching me to be mindful of spending. Growing up during wartime rationing meant we were forced to make do with what little we had, so waste was viewed as unacceptable. My parents always said "a penny saved is a penny earned" and that thought has stuck with me throughout my life. Even now, I try to stick to my rule of thumb of only buying something if there's a real need for it, rather than impulse purchasing. And whenever I'm faced with a decision about whether to spend money, I ask myself if it'll put us into debt – which usually makes me hesitant to take out credit cards. It sounds simple, but it really helps!

I try to plan ahead during sales periods, stocking up on essentials like food, toiletries, and other non-perishable items when prices drop. I'm quite particular about saving money, so I make a point to snag decent deals whenever I spot them. However, I have to admit that impulse buying isn't really my forte – once I know what something is needed, that's usually it! My son always teases me about being too thrifty, but he knows better; we've had our fair share of happy moments splurging together, though. Still, most weeks I stick to my list and avoid unnecessary purchases. It's all about finding a balance between being sensible and enjoying the odd treat every now and then!

I think moderation is key, really. My mum always used to say "don't waste what you're given" – it was a big part of our family motto growing up. As we got older, my husband and I worked hard, so we didn't have much disposable income, let alone food waste. Now that I'm retired, I still try to be mindful of how much I eat, even though I might indulge every now and then. It's all about balance, isn't it? My grandkids love coming round for Sunday roasts, and they'll often ask me to bake some treats like cakes or biscuits. But I make sure they know I only give them a little bit, because too many sweets will spoil everyone! We have a lovely system where I prepare everything ahead of time, measure out ingredients carefully, and cut portions into nice, neat bits. It helps prevent overeating, and I feel quite proud of myself when we finish leftovers without having any rubbish left over. Plus, it gives us something to look forward to during the week – knowing there's always more yummy food waiting for us later!

I think I'd have a hard time going to regular sessions like some people might, but if someone was pushing me towards it, I suppose I could give it a go. If we were meeting once every few weeks, perhaps, where we could talk openly about my thoughts and feelings, I'd be more likely to open up. It sounds too much work otherwise! But I'm not sure how involved I'd need to be; just having someone to listen and offer advice sometimes is what I value most – being able to share my concerns, knowing there's someone who'll hear them out and try to make sense of things. As long as it wasn't too... intense or intrusive, I suppose I could manage that kind of thing.

I think prevention is key here, really. For me, setting a daily routine helps. Up until my retirement, I had a pretty structured workday, so establishing a morning rhythm was crucial. Now that I'm fully retired, sticking to those habits has been helpful – getting up early, having some breakfast, taking a walk... It gives me a sense of purpose, you know? Also, prioritising leisure activities that bring me joy is essential. Whether it's tending to my garden or curling up with a good book, making time for these things ensures I don't feel like I'm wasting away. And, of course, spending time with loved ones does wonders for my mental health too! Another thing that works for me is being mindful of my limits. If I start feeling drained or exhausted, I take a step back, have a cuppa, and remind myself that rest is okay. I used to be one of those people who thought there should always be something going on; now I appreciate the value of downtime. It's funny how our lives go, isn't it? What worked before might not work afterwards! But what I've found is that embracing flexibility and accepting that it's alright to say "no" to certain requests allows me to preserve my

Getting a good night's rest is essential, isn't it? To be honest, I think my evenings have become quite predictable now. I try to wind down before bed by having a cuppa tea and some quiet time – perhaps browsing through a nice book or watching a gentle programme on telly until around 9 pm. Then, once I'm ready for bed, I make sure to stick to a regular bedtime routine, like brushing my teeth, changing into comfy PJs, and settling into bed by 10 pm. It might sound simple, but I find that consistency helps me feel more rested during the day. And of course, getting plenty of fresh air outside, even if just taking short strolls, does wonders for clearing one's mind! Now, give me a good mattress and a decent blanket, and I should be all set for a peaceful slumber!

Well, since my grandkids come to visit regularly now, we like to make a game out of our outdoor time together. We go for bike rides along the canal towpath near my house – it's lovely scenery and gets us all some fresh air. When the weather permits, we have picnics there too; I bring sandwiches, fruit, and sometimes even bake something sweet. When the weather isn't cooperating, I might take them to the local park for a walk, play a bit of frisbee, or simply sit and chat while watching the world go by. It's wonderful having those moments of connection with them. If I'm feeling more energetic, I'll join them at the community centre where they participate in sports classes – I try to cheer them on! But honestly, most days are spent tending to my little patch of garden, making sure everything is growing nicely...

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned sort, but I always try to weigh my options based on what makes sense in terms of value for money and how much effort is required. When faced with decisions like replacing our washing machine, for instance, I think about whether there's still some life left in the current one, rather than immediately going out and buying something brand-new. If we're due for a replacement anyway soon, maybe it's worth waiting until prices drop or looking into second-hand options. On the other hand, if it's causing too many issues and wasting water/electricity, perhaps it's better to bite the bullet and invest in a more efficient model sooner rather than later. A bit of common sense usually helps me make a decision, along with considering factors such as any remaining warranty periods and reviews from others who have used similar machines.

I think incorporating feedback is a vital part of any solution. You know, I believe that people generally respond better to constructive criticism because it shows we're invested in improving rather than being told what to do simply out of authority. When someone takes the trouble to share specific concerns, it makes me realize there might be an issue worth addressing, even if it isn't something I'd initially considered myself. So, my approach tends to listen attentively, ask clarifying questions where necessary – making sure I truly grasp what was meant – before considering how best to implement changes based on those insights gained from feedback. It requires me to reflect upon my actions, pinpoint areas needing improvement, and devise thoughtful adjustments accordingly. This process helps refine our methods, allowing us to move forward more efficiently while continually striving for growth.

I think my approach is quite traditional, really. When faced with a complex issue, I like to break it down into smaller parts and consider each element separately before looking at how they all fit together. It helps me to make sense of the bigger picture, you know? A bit like taking apart an engine to figure out why something isn't running properly – once I have everything laid bare, I find it easier to identify what's gone wrong and how to fix it. Of course, sometimes you need to trust your instincts too; otherwise, analysis paralysis might set in! But generally, I'm a straight-thinking sort who likes to look at facts rather than jumping to conclusions. Does that sound sensible to you?

I like to take a step back and think it out before making decisions or taking action. When faced with something unfamiliar or uncertain, I try breaking it down into smaller parts and considering different viewpoints – what others might say, how my actions could impact those involved, and whether there's more information needed to make an informed choice. Sometimes, having a cuppa tea while sitting quietly helps me gather my thoughts too!

I think identifying areas for growth is essential for me because it helps me be a better version of myself. For instance, since retiring, my main concern has been staying mentally sharp. With some cognitive decline due to age, keeping my mind engaged and challenged comes first priority now – perhaps more books on history or science topics. Sometimes, too, I might find my memory isn't what it used to be so having digital calendars, reminders set up on my tablet helps me remember things like doctor's appointments and birthdays. It makes sense that with everything changing, adapting to those changes becomes vital to move forward effectively, which sometimes requires taking courses or getting guidance from experts. Overall, being open to personal development allows me to make progress despite my limitations and live an enjoyable retirement.

I think my approach is quite simple, really. You know, I'm a bit like my grandmother used to say, "A spoonful of sugar helps the medicine go down." For me, taking something new comes down to breaking it into manageable steps, getting some support along the way, and being prepared for any bumps. If someone explains it clearly and shows me how it works, I find it much easier to grasp what's going on. It's all about finding that balance between being open to new ideas and feeling secure enough to take control of myself. And I must admit, having my grandsons around has been a great help – they're always eager to show me these new-fangled gadgets and gizmos! But yes, I suppose one could say I'm a gradual learner, and once I feel I have a handle on things, I become more confident.

I make sure to fit it into my daily routine. Evenings before bed is usually dedicated to me having a cuppa and getting lost in a novel or some history book. It's something I cherish, helps take my mind off things and relaxes me ready for the night ahead. Sometimes, during the day if I'm feeling stuck or need clarity, I'll break out an encyclopedia or a biography to spark more discussion. Family gatherings like Sunday lunches always involve swapping tales of our lives so I try to incorporate a bit of reading material in there too! The grandkids love helping me digitise family photo albums which is quite therapeutic – we have lovely chats while doing that together. Anyway, finding time has become easier now that the kids aren't at university anymore; still got plenty of free time left though!

I love discovering new authors and genres! You know, my son actually introduced me to a lot of contemporary writers, like Kate Atkinson and Elizabeth Strout. His wife is an avid reader too, so we'd have lively debates about what was good and what wasn't. But before them, I used to rely heavily on word-of-mouth recommendations from friends and family members who shared similar tastes. In fact, one of my all-time favourite books is "The Remains of the Day" by Kazuo Ishiguro – my late husband bought it for our anniversary, and it completely changed my perspective on life. The way he recommended it made me realize how much I had been missing out on great literature! As for finding out about lesser-known authors, I usually stumble upon them while browsing through second-hand bookshops or charity stores. There's something special about holding a vintage edition of a classic novel that really draws me in. Occasionally, I'll pick up a magazine or journal related to history or current events, which might introduce me to someone like Hilary Mantel or Doris Lessing. These discoveries always excite me because I feel like there's still so much more to read and explore! And, I must say, getting older has given me even greater appreciation

I love listening to music! When I'm having a relaxing bath, I find myself singing along to the lyrics. Or sometimes I'll be sewing, ironing, or cooking, and the gentle tunes will match my pace perfectly. It really helps me unwind and focus. Occasionally, I might hum a tune while I'm tending to my garden – there's something therapeutic about watching those plants respond to some soothing melodies too. But generally speaking, most evenings spent unwinding involve putting on some calming classical music and enjoying a cuppa tea before settling into bed.

I find them quite helpful really, especially since my eyesight isn't what it used to be. I listen during my daily commute or while doing some chores. It's lovely being able to multitask a bit more efficiently. As long as there aren't too many complicated instructions or technical terms, I'm happy to follow along. Sometimes I might rewind a segment if I didn't catch something, so I like podcasts and audiobooks that have transcripts available. My grandson is always teasing me about how much he knows because I only seem to remember bits and pieces – but I think it's better than nothing!

I'm quite fond of staying active, even though my body might protest a bit more now than it used to! *laughs* My main thing is gardening, really. There's something so satisfying about getting out into nature and watching everything come alive. It's therapeutic, you know? And, of course, growing your own veggies and herbs makes me feel like I'm contributing to myself rather than relying on supermarkets all the time. When I have some free time, I'll usually put on some music and work on a puzzle – crosswords or jigsaw pieces – to keep my mind sharp. Sometimes I'll take a walk along our local canal path or visit the nearby park. The fresh air does wonders for me! Occasionally, we'll arrange little gatherings with friends, perhaps for dinner parties or a game night, which always brings a lot of joy. Nothing too strenuous, just enjoying each other's company. Overall, being moderately active is key for keeping me going these days. Simple pleasures make such a big difference, don't you think?

I find solitude quite lovely. There's something comforting about having some time all to myself where I can simply relax and recharge. A nice cup of tea, a good book, and my little patch of garden to tend to – there's nothing like it! Of course, being an introvert at heart, I appreciate quieter moments, rather than big gatherings or loud crowds. But even when spending time with loved ones is involved, I feel most happy when we're chatting quietly together, sharing stories and laughter, rather than getting caught up in grand gestures or fuss. It's the simple pleasures in life that bring me joy, really.

I must admit, I'm a bit hesitant when trying new foods, especially exotic ones. But my grandkids have always encouraged me to step out of my comfort zone. They'll say something like, "Nana, come try some somethings we found!" And I suppose that gets me interested. If I know someone is cooking for us, I appreciate being introduced to dishes that aren't traditional British food – helps make mealtime more enjoyable. The first thing I look for is whether there's any sort of familiarity, such as vegetables or meat types used, which makes it easier for me to feel more secure. Once I take one bite, I tend to stick with what tastes okay; sometimes I ask them what else they think works well together so maybe find another dish to share instead.

I think creating variety is key to keeping my mind engaged and avoiding boredom. For me, a mix of structured and unstructured activities works best. In terms of hobbies like gardening, I find that spending some days tending to specific plants or projects, and others focusing on different areas of the garden, really helps keep things interesting. It keeps me on my toes, so to speak! Reading-wise, I try to vary between historical fiction novels, which are great escapists, and more factual books on topics like nature or history – these allow me to broaden my horizons and gain insight into subjects I might otherwise overlook. And of course, there's always baking! Trying out new recipes or experimenting with different flavors and ingredients provides an enjoyable creative outlet. Plus, having family gatherings where we share our latest creations together makes it all worthwhile. Lastly, staying involved with local groups and attending events has been fantastic; whether it's watching a play at the theatre or joining a village fete committee, I love being part of something bigger than myself and meeting fellow enthusiasts who share similar passions. Overall, mixing it up ensures I never become too complacent and allows me to recharge my batteries for whatever comes next.

I'm quite particular about keeping track of my free time, dear. I have a little notebook where I jot down what I plan to do each weekend, along with any notes or reminders I need to remember. It helps me make sure I fit everything in and avoid feeling too busy or stressed. Occasionally, I'll create a rough itinerary with dates and timings so I know exactly how much time is allocated to each activity – be it tending to the garden or attending the local library meeting. My granddaughter even likes to draw pictures alongside mine; it makes us laugh! Of course, some weeks will inevitably slip away, but overall, having a record has been helpful for staying organised and making the most out of my precious free time.

I'm a bit old-school, I suppose! *laughs* For me, it's all about having routines and sticking to them. My daily planner is my best friend – I know exactly where everything should be and what's due each day. It helps me prioritize and make sure nothing falls through the cracks. Of course, it's not foolproof, but it works for me. In terms of digital tools, I have a simple setup: my phone has reminders set up for recurring tasks like grocery shopping and bill payments, and I use an email client to manage correspondence with the kids and grandkids. Occasionally, I'll look up something interesting online, usually while taking a break during one of our afternoon cups of tea. And let's say I find myself getting sidetracked sometimes... *chuckles*, so I rely on writing notes to remind myself why I was going somewhere before I got lost! To keep my paperwork tidy, I designate specific folders and labels for different documents, making sure they're easy to access when needed. Simple habits, really. What worked since I started teaching and later, running a front desk job still work for me today! *smiles*.

Before leaving my house, I make myself a nice cup of tea, usually Earl Grey, and have a bit of toast or some biscuits while I gather any essentials like my handbag, wallet, and keys. Then, I take a quick glance out the window to check the weather forecast. It helps me plan what kind of coat or umbrella I might need. Sometimes, I'll jot down notes for the day – reminders or to-do lists – on a little notebook. And if there's something specific I'd rather avoid talking about or discussing publicly, I give those topics a brief thought beforehand so I'm prepared to handle any conversations politely and constructively. After all, one never knows who we're meeting or where our paths will cross!

I think one of my proudest achievements has been making our little place more eco-friendly while keeping costs manageable. We replaced most of those dreadful old incandescent bulbs with LED ones a couple of years back, which made quite a difference already. Now we're looking into getting some insulation done in the attic – something I'd always meant to sort out, really. And I've started using draught excluder stuff like draft stoppers and door snakes along the gaps. It might seem simple, but it all adds up, don't you think? We've also taken to turning lights off when not needed and reducing hot water usage whenever possible – every little counts! Our grandkids helped us put up some lovely solar panels last summer; now our electricity bill is lower, and they love knowing they contributed to going green too. My late husband was quite keen on recycling before he...well, anyway, his legacy lives on here. Oh, and I almost forgot – since becoming semi-retiree, I make sure to tidy up regularly so heat isn't wasted heating an unoccupied room! It may sound trivial, but these tiny actions have become part of daily routines for me, and I find great satisfaction in contributing even slightly to preserving what's precious. What do you

To be honest, I'm a bit hesitant about all these new-fangled gadgets. I mean, I know some people swear by them, but I find myself getting frustrated enough as it is keeping track of everything else. If something breaks or malfunctions, I'd rather have someone to sort out the technical bits, thank you very much! Still, my grandkids seem quite taken with their Alexa thingy, so I suppose I'll give it another go eventually. But for now, I think I'll stick with what I know – a simple radio and television will suffice, please.

Well, you know, I think prioritising is quite simple once you break it down, isn't it? For me, it comes down to what's most pressing and essential. If something affects my daily life directly, like keeping my grandkids safe and happy, that takes precedence. Family always has been a top priority. If there's something related to my health or wellbeing – managing my chronic conditions – I'll make sure those needs come first too. And of course, looking forward to retirement and making the most of my golden years is important to me, so I try to balance all these aspects accordingly. It's never easy, mind you; sometimes one thing overlaps another, but generally speaking, common sense helps guide my decision-making process.
